medications.drug_name: perindopril (ACE inhibitor) — CEASE immediately and permanently
medications.indication_rationale: Perindopril is the causative agent of this bradykinin-mediated angioedema. It must be stopped immediately and never resumed; all ACE inhibitors ('-pril' drugs) must be avoided lifelong. Continuing it, or failing to identify it, leaves the driver of the angioedema in place.
contraindications_in_this_case: Is the causative agent for the presenting angioedema — absolutely contraindicated going forward
medications.deprescribing_note: Cease now and permanently; record ACE-inhibitor-induced angioedema as an adverse drug reaction on the patient's record and alert every future prescriber and pharmacist. Avoid all ACE inhibitors lifelong; ARBs carry a small cross-reactivity risk and should be used only on specialist advice.

medications.drug_name: alternative antihypertensive (e.g. a calcium channel blocker such as amlodipine)
medications.drug_class: calcium channel blocker (or other non-ACE-inhibitor antihypertensive class)
medications.indication_rationale: Ongoing blood-pressure control is still required; substitute a different antihypertensive class (commonly a calcium channel blocker) at GP review, chosen by the reviewing clinician. Avoid ACE inhibitors and use ARBs only with caution.

A single stat dose was given in the source note as a safety buffer in case of a mixed histaminergic component. It has limited efficacy in bradykinin-mediated angioedema and does NOT treat or protect the airway; it must never substitute for drug cessation and airway escalation.
medications.dose_route_frequency: prednisolone 50 mg orally, single stat dose (from source note)

medications.indication_rationale: A stat oral antihistamine was given in the source note as a safety buffer for a possible mixed presentation. Antihistamines are ineffective for bradykinin-mediated angioedema and must not delay ceasing the ACE inhibitor or escalating for airway assessment.
medications.dose_route_frequency: cetirizine 10 mg orally, single dose (from source note)

medications.drug_name: adrenaline (autoinjector) — rescue only if airway deteriorates
medications.indication_rationale: Adrenaline is the treatment for anaphylaxis, not for bradykinin-mediated ACE-inhibitor angioedema, and should not be relied upon as the primary treatment here. However, if the airway acutely deteriorates and a mixed/allergic component cannot be excluded, an available adrenaline autoinjector should be used into the outer thigh while 000 is called — it may buy time and is harmless in this rescue context. The autoinjector is pre-dosed (no dose calculation permitted).
medications.dose_route_frequency: adrenaline autoinjector 300 microgram IM outer mid-thigh (device pre-dosed) ONLY if airway compromise or anaphylaxis features develop, while calling 000

medications.indication_rationale: Targeted bradykinin therapies are hospital-only options considered for refractory or airway-threatening ACEi angioedema; they are outside the scope of telehealth or GP management and are noted here only for completeness of the correct escalation pathway.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Never take perindopril or any other ACE inhibitor ('-pril') again; ensure it is recorded as an adverse drug reaction and told to every doctor and pharmacist.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Re-exposure to any ACE inhibitor risks recurrent, potentially airway-threatening angioedema; a flagged record prevents accidental re-prescription.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the partner present and observing overnight; rest propped upright and watch for the airway warning signs.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Bradykinin angioedema can shift or worsen over 24-48 hours; a present observer enables an immediate 000 call if the airway deteriorates.

patient_education_points.point: The new blood-pressure tablet caused fluid to leak into the deep tissues of your face — this is a reaction to the medicine, and stopping it is the main treatment.
This is different from an allergy: antihistamines, steroids and even adrenaline don't work well for this type of swelling, so stopping the drug and watching your airway are what matter most.
Never take perindopril or any '-pril' medicine again, and make sure it is added to your allergy/adverse-reaction list.
The swelling usually settles over 24-72 hours after stopping the drug, but it can shift or worsen during the first day, so stay watchful.
Call 000 immediately (do not drive) for any voice change, throat tightness or lump-in-throat feeling, trouble swallowing your saliva, tongue swelling, or noisy or difficult breathing.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person assessment today, then GP review the next morning for blood-pressure management and re-evaluation of the swelling
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: airway and oropharynx, extent and progression of the swelling, blood pressure, and selection of a non-ACE-inhibitor antihypertensive
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any voice change, throat tightness, difficulty swallowing, tongue swelling or breathing difficulty — call 000 immediately

must_include_items: Recognise angioedema affecting the airway region (not simple facial swelling)
Identify the recently started ACE inhibitor ('-pril') and instruct that it be CEASED immediately
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment because the airway cannot be examined via telehealth
Give mandatory, specific airway safety-netting with an immediate 000 trigger (and ambulance, not self-transport)
Advise lifelong avoidance of all ACE inhibitors and recording it as an adverse drug reaction
acceptable_alternatives: Escalating directly to an emergency department (T4) for airway-capable assessment is a safe, slightly more cautious alternative to urgent same-day GP/urgent-care review
Giving a stat antihistamine and/or corticosteroid as an adjunct is acceptable provided it does NOT replace ceasing the ACE inhibitor or the airway escalation
errors_of_omission: Failing to identify and cease the ACE inhibitor
Failing to give airway safety-netting with a 000 trigger
Being reassured by the currently clear airway and advising routine GP follow-up only
Not recognising that the swelling is in the airway region
errors_of_commission: Treating this as a simple allergy and relying on adrenaline / antihistamine / corticosteroid as the definitive treatment
Advising the patient to continue the ACE inhibitor
Advising the patient to drive themselves to hospital if the airway deteriorates
Substituting an ARB without cautioning about cross-reactivity
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (a) ceasing the ACE inhibitor AND (b) mandatory airway safety-netting with a 000 trigger. Recommending routine-only GP follow-up without same-day in-person airway assessment, or relying on adrenaline/antihistamine/steroid as the definitive fix, is a failure.
